quotes.verbatim_quote:
This exercise gets more into the real world as this type thing occors in many jazz phrases.
      Apply to any two adjacent strings using the double stop of your choice. Do not let notes ring!
quotes.why_load_bearing: Establishes the chapter's premise that consecutive same-direction strokes across adjacent strings are an essential jazz vocabulary technique, with a strict no-ringing constraint.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Same as previous exercise but in reverse. Do not let notes ring!
quotes.why_load_bearing: Pairs the reverse-direction drill with the same no-ringing rule, making muting a defining requirement of the consecutive-stroke technique.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
It is necessary to play the above exercises evenly . At this stage, precision of the 8th
   notes is more important than speed.
quotes.why_load_bearing: States the method's core practice rule for this stage: evenness and rhythmic precision are prerequisites to tempo.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
I have found when playing triplets with a repeating pattern such as a triad or some other
        pattern, it is better to break the rule. Instead of playing the exercise like this,
quotes.why_load_bearing: Articulates Bruno's signature exception: strict alternation is suspended for repeating triplet patterns like triad arpeggios.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
use three "down-strokes" followed by three "up-strokes". Apply to any three note chord on
     any three adjacent strings. This exercise is designed to practice three consecutive strokes
     in the same direction.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the specific picking pattern that operationalizes the broken-rule approach for triadic triplets across three strings.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
however, if I had to play a triplet phrase in the context of a line and not in an exercise,
            I would adhere to the rule. The following phrase illustrates my point.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Clarifies that the exception is exercise-only; in actual melodic lines, strict alternation returns, distinguishing drill technique from playing technique.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
This exercise requires you to reverse direction on the same string. A common stroke that happens with
                                                                                                 with repeated
                                                                                                      repeated notes
                                                                                                               notes
Apply this exercise to any four note chord anywhere on the guitar.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Introduces the same-string reversal as a distinct sub-skill within consecutive strokes, tied to repeated-note phrasing on chord voicings.

quotes.verbatim_quote: or like this with 16th notes. Pay close attention to the accents.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Flags accent placement as the load-bearing musical feature when the broken-rule pattern is recast into sixteenth notes.
